title: Building the LC3 Club Website
excerpt: How we designed and built the LC3 Lowcode Cloud Club website from scratch — the stack, the decisions, and what we learned along the way.
content:
Every club needs a home on the web. For LC3 — the Lowcode Cloud Club at the College of Southern Nevada — that meant building something that actually represents who we are: modern, clean, and functional.

## Why We Built It from Scratch

We could have used a website builder or a template, but that felt off-brand. LC3 is a club about building real things with real tech — it didn't make sense to outsource our own presence. So we built it ourselves, and treated it as a real project.

## The Stack

We landed on a stack that's practical and industry-relevant:

- **Next.js 15** (App Router) — server components, dynamic routes, and fast rendering out of the box
- **TypeScript** — strict typing across the entire codebase
- **Tailwind CSS v4** — utility-first styling with dark mode support
- **Cloudinary** — image hosting and gallery management
- **Railway** — deployment and hosting

All content — members, events, projects, blog posts, resources — is stored as JSON files and managed through a custom admin dashboard. No external CMS needed.

## The Admin Dashboard

One of the more interesting parts of the project was building the admin panel. It handles:

- **Members** — add, edit, reorder, and manage advisor/officer/member tiers
- **Events** — create upcoming and past events with RSVP tracking
- **Projects** — manage the project portfolio with status badges (In Progress, Completed, Open to Contributors)
- **Blog** — write and publish posts in Markdown
- **Gallery** — upload photos to Cloudinary with event labels and captions
- **Resources** — curate a learning library grouped by category
- **Site Settings** — meeting info, social links, announcement banner

Everything updates live — no rebuild required.

## Design Decisions

A few things we were intentional about:

**Dark mode first.** Every component was designed with both light and dark themes in mind from the start, not bolted on afterward.

**Animated SVG illustrations.** Each page has a custom hand-crafted SVG icon in the header — camera for Gallery, calendar for Events, terminal for Projects, and so on. They add personality without slowing the page down.

**Flip cards for members.** The members page uses 3D flip cards — the front shows name and role, the back reveals skills, majors, and social links. It's a small touch that makes browsing the team more engaging.

**No external dependencies for content.** Everything lives in the repo as JSON. This keeps the site fast, the data portable, and the setup simple for anyone who joins the team.

## What We Learned

Building your own club website is a surprisingly good learning project. You end up touching a bit of everything — routing, API design, authentication, image handling, form validation, deployment. It's the kind of full-stack experience that's hard to get from tutorials alone.

The site is open source and continues to evolve. If you're an LC3 member and want to contribute, check out the repo on GitHub.

---

*Built by LC3 — Lowcode Cloud Club · College of Southern Nevada*
